Author: Anand Satheesh Kumar Nair
Title: Coding at the Speed of Thought: My Experience with Cursor IDE and Claude Sonnet
Text:
Coding at the Speed of Thought: My Experience with Cursor IDE and Claude Sonnet
Introduction: A New Era of Software Development
We’re at a turning point in how software gets built. Not long ago, being a developer meant spending hours (or days) writing and debugging lines of code by hand. But in this new world — where Large Language Models (LLMs) are seamlessly integrated into your IDE — the game has changed.
Cursor IDE is one of the best examples of what modern development looks like. It’s an AI-native coding environment that doesn’t just autocomplete — it thinks with you. Especially for greenfield projects, where there’s little legacy baggage and a wide creative runway, I’ve found that developing with Cursor has taken my productivity to another level.
Instead of wrestling with boilerplate or Googling every third line, I’m bootstrapping apps in days that would’ve taken me weeks before. And with the integration of powerful LLMs like Claude Sonnet, the IDE feels more like a pair programmer than a traditional tool.
But it’s not perfect — and that’s okay. In this piece, I want to walk through what it’s like to actually build in Cursor IDE, where it shines, where it struggles, and why I believe tools like this represent the future of development.
Why I Tried Cursor IDE
Like many developers, I’m always on the lookout for tools that can make me faster — not just in typing, but in thinking, building, and shipping. The idea of an AI-native IDE intrigued me, especially when I started hearing how Cursor integrates directly with LLMs and supports agent-like behavior for writing and understanding code.
I was particularly interested in how it could help with greenfield development — situations where you’re starting fresh and speed matters. I decided to put Cursor to the test by building a few Python applications from scratch, integrating it with Claude Sonnet as my underlying model. The results were… honestly, eye-opening.
Cursor wasn’t just suggesting lines of code — it was helping shape the structure of the application, proposing functions, and even reasoning through logic. Within hours, I had working prototypes. Things that used to take me a week to put together were suddenly up and running in a couple of days.
It felt less like coding line-by-line and more like guiding the system to build what I had in mind. That shift in workflow was both powerful and strangely freeing.
Cursor IDE + Claude Sonnet — A Powerful Duo
One of the standout features of Cursor IDE is its ability to plug into various LLMs and act more like an AI teammate than a traditional code editor. I chose to pair it with Claude Sonnet — a model known for its strong reasoning capabilities — and the results were pretty impressive.
The integration feels fluid. You can highlight code, explain what it does, ask the model to improve it, refactor it, or even generate new functions based on a comment or a vague idea. And the best part? It doesn’t just throw random code at you — it tries to understand the context. With Claude Sonnet behind the scenes, that understanding felt more thoughtful, especially when compared to models that just autocomplete based on patterns.
Of course, it’s not perfect. Early prompts didn’t always give the best output. But what’s fascinating is that it gets better the more you use it — like it’s learning how you work. As I iterated on ideas, Cursor + Claude started generating more aligned suggestions, reducing the back-and-forth I usually have with traditional IDEs or ChatGPT-style coding.
It really shines when you’re in flow: writing some logic, prompting the agent to fill in the blanks, refining the structure, and repeating. This agentic workflow — where the IDE feels like it’s participating in the design and build process — is something I hadn’t quite experienced before. And it’s incredibly productive.
What Works Really Well
If there’s one word I’d use to describe development with Cursor IDE, it’s speed. Not just the speed of typing code — but the speed of ideation, prototyping, and execution.
🚀 1. Bootstrapping is a breeze
When starting from scratch, Cursor becomes a superpower. I’ve built full Python applications with clean architecture in just a couple of days — something that used to take me a week or more. It’s especially helpful when you’re building CRUD-style apps, scripts, or quick backend services. Cursor handles boilerplate like a champ, letting you focus on what actually matters.
🧠 2. Agent-like behavior
Cursor’s agentic capabilities make it feel more like a collaborator than a tool. Ask it to “refactor this function,” and it doesn’t just change names — it restructures, renames, and even leaves comments. When paired with Claude Sonnet, it has a surprising ability to reason through your intentions, not just mirror your syntax.
🔁 3. It improves over time
At first, some of Cursor’s responses can be hit or miss. But as you work with it — giving feedback, trying different prompts, revisiting flows — the output becomes sharper. It starts aligning better with your style, your patterns, and your domain.
🧪 4. It encourages testing (and that’s a good thing)
Because the agent sometimes overreaches — rewriting more than you asked for — it encourages you to build proper test coverage. Ironically, this has made my codebases safer, because I’m forced to validate changes instead of assuming everything works after a tweak.
Where It Struggles
As much as I love working with Cursor IDE, it’s not without its quirks. Like any tool riding the early wave of AI-native development, there are a few areas where it still has room to grow.
🏗️ 1. Existing Codebases are Hard
Cursor shines in greenfield projects, but when it comes to navigating legacy code, the magic fades a bit. The agent doesn’t always grasp why things were coded a certain way. This lack of historical context makes it risky to ask for deep refactors or structural changes in mature codebases — it might suggest something that looks cleaner, but breaks subtle business logic.
🤖 2. Overeager Edits
Sometimes, Cursor does more than you ask for. You might request a small change, and it’ll rewrite an entire block or modify unrelated functions. This can be helpful… or dangerous, especially without robust test coverage. It reinforces the need to always review changes closely and treat AI suggestions as starting points — not gospel.
🎯 3. Prompt Sensitivity
The results are heavily dependent on how you ask. Early prompts, especially vague or underspecified ones, don’t always return the right output. But with time and clearer instructions, the model improves. It’s a learning curve — both for the user and the agent.
Despite these rough edges, none of them have been deal-breakers. In fact, they’ve made me more intentional in how I communicate with the IDE — a skill that’s becoming just as important as traditional coding.
Tips for Getting the Most Out of Cursor
Working with an AI-native IDE like Cursor is a bit like onboarding a new teammate — the better you communicate, the better the outcomes. Here are a few things I’ve learned that helped me get the most from it:
✍️ 1. Write clear, structured prompts
The more specific you are, the better the results. Instead of “fix this,” say “refactor this function to use a dictionary instead of multiple if-else statements.” Think of it like talking to a junior developer who’s very smart, but still needs context.
🧪 2. Use test cases as guardrails
Since Cursor can sometimes touch more than you intended, having unit tests or integration tests in place is crucial. They act as a safety net and make it easier to validate the changes it suggests.
💾 3. Commit early, commit often
Version control becomes even more important when working with an AI agent. Sometimes it makes brilliant improvements. Other times… not so much. Committing regularly gives you a way to roll back quickly if things go sideways.
♻️ 4. Iterate with the AI, don’t just accept the first response
Treat Cursor like a pair programming session. Ask follow-ups. Refine prompts. Rerun generations. Often the second or third try nails it better than the first.
📈 5. Be patient — it gets better
Cursor learns as you go. It starts recognizing your style, your patterns, and the way you structure code. Early on, the results may feel uneven, but they improve quickly if you stick with it.
Final Thoughts
Cursor IDE isn’t just a code editor — it’s a glimpse into the future of how we’ll build software. When paired with a powerful LLM like Claude Sonnet, it becomes an intelligent assistant that helps you move faster, think more clearly, and focus on solving actual problems rather than just writing syntax.
For greenfield development, it’s a game-changer. I’ve seen my timelines shrink dramatically, and the process feels less like coding and more like collaborating. Sure, it has its rough edges — especially when dealing with existing codebases or overly eager auto-edits — but those are trade-offs I’m more than happy to manage.
The experience also reinforces an important shift: the future of development isn’t just about how fast you can write code, it’s about how well you can guide and communicate with your AI partner. Cursor, Windsurf, and tools like them are opening up new workflows that are more creative, fluid, and frankly — more fun.
If you’re curious about what it feels like to develop at the speed of thought, give Cursor IDE a try. Just don’t forget your test cases 😉
Written by Anand Nair (also published as Anand S Nair / Anand Satheesh Kumar Nair).
